lines.greek: Ἀργείων παρὰ νηυσὶ χαρίζετο ἱερὰ ῥέζων
Τροίῃ ἐν εὐρείῃ; τί νύ οἱ τόσον ὠδύσαο, Ζεῦ;
τὴν δʼ ἀπαμειβόμενος προσέφη νεφεληγερέτα Ζεύς·
τέκνον ἐμόν, ποῖόν σε ἔπος φύγεν ἕρκος ὀδόντων.
πῶς ἂν ἔπειτʼ Ὀδυσῆος ἐγὼ θείοιο λαθοίμην,
ὃς περὶ μὲν νόον ἐστὶ βροτῶν, περὶ δʼ ἱρὰ θεοῖσιν
ἀθανάτοισιν ἔδωκε, τοὶ οὐρανὸν εὐρὺν ἔχουσιν;
ἀλλὰ Ποσειδάων γαιήοχος ἀσκελὲς αἰεὶ
Κύκλωπος κεχόλωται, ὃν ὀφθαλμοῦ ἀλάωσεν,
ἀντίθεον Πολύφημον, ὅου κράτος ἐστὶ μέγιστον
πᾶσιν Κυκλώπεσσι· Θόωσα δέ μιν τέκε νύμφη,
Φόρκυνος θυγάτηρ ἁλὸς ἀτρυγέτοιο μέδοντος,
ἐν σπέσσι γλαφυροῖσι Ποσειδάωνι μιγεῖσα.
ἐκ τοῦ δὴ Ὀδυσῆα Ποσειδάων ἐνοσίχθων
οὔ τι κατακτείνει, πλάζει δʼ ἀπὸ πατρίδος αἴης.
ἀλλʼ ἄγεθʼ, ἡμεῖς οἵδε περιφραζώμεθα πάντες
νόστον, ὅπως ἔλθῃσι· Ποσειδάων δὲ μεθήσει
ὃν χόλον· οὐ μὲν γάρ τι δυνήσεται ἀντία πάντων
ἀθανάτων ἀέκητι θεῶν ἐριδαινέμεν οἶος.
τὸν δʼ ἠμείβετʼ ἔπειτα θεά, γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη·

interlinear: of-the-Argives beside the-ships gratify you, sacred-things performing
in-Troy the-wide? why now at-him so-much wast-thou-wroth, Zeus?
her then answering addressed cloud-gathering Zeus:
'child my, what-sort-of a word escaped you the-barrier of-teeth!
how then of-Odysseus could I, divine, be-forgetful,
who beyond indeed in-mind is of-mortals, and-beyond sacred-things to-the-gods
immortal gave, who heaven wide hold?
but Poseidon the-earth-holder, unceasingly, ever
at-the-Cyclops is-enraged, whom of-eye he-blinded,
godlike Polyphemus, whose might is greatest
among-all Cyclopes; and-Thoosa him bore, a-nymph,
of-Phorcys daughter, of-the-sea unresting the-ruler,
in caves hollow with-Poseidon having-lain.
from that indeed Odysseus Poseidon the-earth-shaker
not at-all slays, but-drives-wandering from his-native land.
but come, we here let-us-take-counsel, all,
of-his-homecoming, how he-may-come; and-Poseidon will-let-go
his wrath; for not indeed at-all will-he-be-able against all
the-immortals, against-the-will of-gods, to-contend alone.
him then answered the-goddess, bright-eyed Athena:
literal: please you, offering sacrifice beside the ships of the Argives
in the wide land of Troy? Why then, Zeus, wast thou so wroth with him?'
Then Zeus the cloud-gatherer answered her and said:
'My child, what a word has escaped the barrier of your teeth!
How then could I forget divine Odysseus,
who is beyond all mortals in mind, and beyond all has given
sacrifice to the immortal gods who hold broad heaven?
Nay, it is Poseidon, the earth-enfolder, who without ceasing
is enraged at the Cyclops, whom Odysseus blinded of his eye—
godlike Polyphemus, whose might is greatest
among all the Cyclopes; the nymph Thoosa bore him,
daughter of Phorcys, ruler of the unresting sea,
when she had lain with Poseidon in the hollow caves.
From that time forth Poseidon the earth-shaker
does not slay Odysseus, but drives him wandering from his native land.
But come, let all of us here take counsel together
of his homecoming, that he may return; and Poseidon will let go
his anger, for he will in no way be able, against all
the immortal gods, to strive alone in their despite.'
Then the goddess, bright-eyed Athena, answered him:
translations.elizabethan:
"Did not Odysseus by the Argive ships
pour offerings out in Troy's wide land? Why then,
O Zeus, against him hast thou kept this wrath?"
So spake she. Answered then the lord of clouds:
"My child, what word hath leapt thy teeth's white fence?
How should I e'er forget the god-born man,
Odysseus, first of mortals in his mind,
who most of all gave offerings to the gods
that hold the wide of heaven? Nay, 'tis he
who girds the earth, Poseidon, still enraged
without surcease, because Odysseus put
the Cyclops' eye to darkness, Polypheme,
the godlike, mightiest of all his kind:
him did the nymph Thoosa bear, the child
of Phorcys, lord of the unharvested brine,
when in the hollow caves she lay with him
who shakes the shore. For this Poseidon drives
Odysseus wandering far, yet slays him not
outright. But come, let all of us take thought
for his return; and he shall loose his wrath,
for never can he stand alone against
the will of all the deathless gods."
translations.modernist:
"Did Odysseus not make his offerings by the Argive ships in the wide land of Troy? Why are you so angry with him, Zeus?"

Zeus, who gathers the clouds, answered her. "My child. What have you let past your teeth? How could I forget Odysseus? No mortal has a better mind. No one gave more to the gods who hold the wide sky. It is Poseidon. He will not let go. He is angry about the Cyclops, whose eye Odysseus put out. Polyphemus. The strongest of all the Cyclopes. The nymph Thoosa bore him. She was the daughter of Phorcys, who rules the barren sea. She lay with Poseidon in the hollow caves. Ever since, Poseidon does not kill Odysseus. He only keeps him from home. But come. Let us all think how he can get back. Poseidon will give up his anger. He cannot fight all the gods alone."
translations.storybook:
"But Zeus, didn't Odysseus give you lovely offerings beside the ships at Troy? Why are you so cross with him?"

Then Zeus, who gathers up the clouds, answered her. "My dear child! What a thing to let slip past your teeth! How could I ever forget Odysseus? Why, no man alive is as clever as he is, and no one ever gave the gods more presents! No, no. It isn't me who is angry. It is Poseidon, the god who holds the earth. He simply will not let it go. You see, Odysseus once poked out the single eye of the Cyclops, a great giant called Polyphemus, the strongest of all the one-eyed Cyclopes. And Polyphemus happens to be Poseidon's very own son! So ever since then, Poseidon will not quite kill Odysseus, but he keeps blowing him this way and that, far from home. But come now! Let us all put our heads together and think how to bring him home. Poseidon will have to let his temper cool, for he cannot go on quarreling all alone against every other god."

murray.text: How should I, then, forget godlike Odysseus, who is beyond all mortals in wisdom, and beyond all has paid sacrifice to the immortal gods, who hold broad heaven? Nay, it is Poseidon, the earth-enfolder, who is ever filled with stubborn wrath because of the Cyclops, whom Odysseus blinded of his eye—

even the godlike Polyphemus, whose might is greatest among all the Cyclopes; and the nymph Thoosa bore him, daughter of Phorcys who rules over the unresting sea; for in the hollow caves she lay with Poseidon. From that time forth Poseidon, the earth-shaker,

does not indeed slay Odysseus, but makes him a wanderer from his native land. But come, let us who are here all take thought of his return, that he may come home; and Poseidon will let go his anger, for he will in no wise be able, against all the immortal gods and in their despite, to contend alone.”

Then the goddess, flashing-eyed Athena, answered him: “Father of us all, thou son of Cronos, high above all lords, if indeed this is now well pleasing to the blessed gods, that the wise Odysseus should return to his own home, let us send forth Hermes, the messenger, Argeiphontes,
